---
title: 'SPH6004 Individual Assignment: Predicting ICU Discharge Using Machine Learning'
author: 'Ansel Lim'
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
  bookdown::html_document2:
    toc: true
    number_sections: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(corrplot)

# Helper: load a results CSV, normalise feature_set naming and ordering
load_results <- function(path) {
  df <- read.csv(path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  # unify "candidate" / "all" -> "All Features"
  label_map <- c(candidate = "All Features", all = "All Features",
                 stepwise = "Stepwise", lasso = "Lasso",
                 elastic = "Elastic Net", boruta = "Boruta")
  df$feature_set <- label_map[df$feature_set]
  # enforce consistent order
  desired_order <- c("All Features", "Stepwise", "Lasso", "Elastic Net", "Boruta")
  df <- df[match(desired_order, df$feature_set), ]
  df
}
```

```{r, include=FALSE}
df_original <- read.csv("data/Assignment1_mimic dataset.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
df <- readRDS("data/df.rds") #   after filtering out non-ICU patients and removing unwanted columns, but before train-test-split
df_train <- readRDS("data/train.rds") # after train-test split, missingness-based filtering, imputation of missing values, standardization, and SMOTE
```

Code for this assignment is available on GitHub[^github].

[^github]: <https://github.com/ansellim/SPH6004_individual_assignment_ansel>

# Introduction

## Dataset

The MIMIC-IV (Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care IV) database is a large, freely available real-world electronic medical record data set comprising de-identified health data of patients who stayed in critical care units at the Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center (BIDMC) in Boston, Massachusetts.

The original dataset contains longitudinal data. However, our extracted dataset has already been temporally "flattened". Each record represents a snapshot of a patient's health status at the time of ICU admission, and does not account for the temporal evolution of clinical parameters over the course of an ICU stay. The features available for analysis span the following categories:

- **Demographics:** age, sex, and race
- **Severity of illness:** component scores of the Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA), capturing dysfunction across the respiratory, cardiovascular, coagulation, hepatic, renal, and central nervous systems
- **Vital signs:** heart rate, systolic, diastolic, and mean arterial blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, and oxygen saturation (SpO2), each summarized as the minimum, maximum, and mean
- **Glucose:** minimum, maximum, and mean blood glucose levels
- **Neurological monitoring:** Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) total and component scores (motor, verbal, eye-opening responses)
- **Laboratory data**, comprising:
  - *Hematology:* complete blood count (CBC) including CBC differential counts
  - *Biochemistry:* renal panel/electrolytes, liver function tests, cardiac and muscle enzymes, as well as amylase
  - *Coagulation parameters:* D-dimer, fibrinogen, thrombin time, INR, PT, and PTT
  - *Arterial blood gas (ABG):* oxygen saturation (SO2) and partial pressure of oxygen (PO2)

**Problem statement.** ICU discharge status is a key outcome variable of interest. Predicting ICU discharge would allow ICU clinicians and nursing managers to plan and allocate resources, facilitate step-down care for eligible patients, and provide clinical decision support in terms of prognostication and advancing discharge readiness.

## Dataset preparation

The pre-extracted dataset was preprocessed further.

I restricted my analysis to patients admitted to one of eight recognized intensive care unit (ICU) types: the Medical ICU (MICU), Cardiac Vascular ICU (CVICU), Medical/Surgical ICU (MICU/SICU), Surgical ICU (SICU), Trauma SICU (TSICU), Coronary Care Unit (CCU), Neuro Surgical ICU (Neuro SICU), and the general ICU. After excluding records with missing discharge timestamps, the filtered dataset comprised `r nrow(df)` patients who have ever stayed in the ICU.

I removed identifier columns (`subject_id`, `hadm_id`, and `stay_id`), care unit columns (`first_careunit`, `last_careunit`), timing variables (`intime`, `outtime`), leakage variables (`los`, `deathtime`, `hospital_expire_flag`) and ethically excluded variables (`insurance`, `language`, `marital_status`). I recoded the 33 original race categories into 7 broader groups of Asian, Black, Hispanic/Latino, Native American, White, Other Race, and Unknown.

The dataset was split into a training set (70%) and test set (30%). Features with >10% missing values in the training set are dropped, leaving `r ncol(df_train) -1` out of `r ncol(df) - 1` features in the dataset. These are considered as candidate features for further preprocessing and analysis.

Figure \@ref(fig:missingness) shows the % missingness in each candidate variable in the train and test sets, showing that all the numeric variables have missing values and that the distribution of missingness is similar between test and train sets.

```{r missingness, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Percentage of missing values per candidate variable in the train and test sets.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/missingness_plot_train_test.png")
```

Missing value imputation was performed using median imputation and then applied to the test set. Z-score standardization was fit on the training set and applied to both training and test sets. SMOTENC was applied to the train set to balance the minority class (ICU death), which handles the mix of categorical and numeric features by interpolating synthetic values for numeric features and assigning categories via majority vote among the k nearest neighbors. After preprocessing, the training and test sets were saved for downstream model fitting and evaluation.

# Feature selection

Feature selection was motivated by the presence of correlated features in the dataset (for example, the SOFA component scores; Figure \@ref(fig:sofa-corr)), as well as the following considerations:

1. **Multicollinearity:** Correlated features destabilize model coefficients, add noise, and compromise predictive accuracy and model stability.
2. **Overfitting and Generalization:** Fewer features reduce the risk of overfitting and improve generalization to unseen patients and external settings.
3. **Computational Efficiency:** Dimensionality reduction lowers the cost of model training and inference, which is important for real-time clinical decision support.
4. **Interpretability and Clinical Utility:** A parsimonious model is more interpretable and easier to deploy at the point of care. High-dimensional models may require variables that are unavailable, expensive to collect, or frequently missing, limiting their practical utility.


```{r sofa-corr, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Correlation matrix of SOFA component scores in the training set.", fig.width=3.5, fig.height=3.2}
sofa_vars <- df_train[, grep("^sofa2", names(df_train))]
sofa_labels <- gsub("sofa2_|_24h_max", "", names(sofa_vars))
sofa_labels <- tools::toTitleCase(sofa_labels)
sofa_labels[sofa_labels == "Cns"] <- "Neurological"
sofa_labels[sofa_labels == "Respiration"] <- "Respiratory"
names(sofa_vars) <- sofa_labels
cor_mat <- cor(sofa_vars)
cor_df  <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat))
names(cor_df) <- c("Var1", "Var2", "value")
# keep lower triangle only
cor_df <- cor_df[as.integer(cor_df$Var1) >= as.integer(cor_df$Var2), ]
cor_df$label <- ifelse(cor_df$Var1 == cor_df$Var2, "",
                       sprintf("%.2f", cor_df$value))
lvls <- colnames(cor_mat)
cor_df$Var1 <- factor(cor_df$Var1, levels = lvls)
cor_df$Var2 <- factor(cor_df$Var2, levels = lvls)

ggplot(cor_df, aes(Var2, Var1, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), angle = 90, size = 2.2, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1), name = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.key.height = unit(0.5, "cm"))
```

## Feature selection methods

I used logistic regression to select the right features for the predictive model.

Four feature selection methods were applied:

- *Forward stepwise selection:* A greedy algorithm that starts from a null model and adds one feature at a time, selecting whichever reduces the AIC the most at each step.
- *Lasso (L1 regularization):* Fits logistic regression with an L1 penalty that shrinks coefficients of less important features to exactly zero, performing implicit variable selection.
- *Elastic net (L1 + L2 regularization):* Extends lasso by combining L1 and L2 penalties, with the mixing parameter tuned via cross-validation.
- *Boruta:* A wrapper around random forest that uses an all-relevant feature selection approach. At each iteration, features are classified as confirmed, rejected, or tentative by comparing their importance to that of randomly permuted shadow features; decisions become more definitive as iterations increase.

## What features were selected?

```{r feature-selection-table, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

lr_results <- read.csv("results/logistic_regression_results.csv",
                       stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# only care about showing the feature sets for the feature-selected sets; do not need to show ALL THE CANDIDATE FEATURES.
lr_results <- lr_results %>% filter(feature_set != "all")

# All predictors in df_train (excluding target)
all_features <- setdiff(names(df_train), "icu_death_flag")

# Build a summary table: for each method, list selected and dropped features
fs_table <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Forward Stepwise", "Lasso", "Elastic Net", "Boruta"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

fs_table$`No. Selected` <- lr_results$n_features
fs_table$`Selected Features` <- sapply(lr_results$features, function(f) {
  paste(sort(strsplit(f, ";")[[1]]), collapse = ", ")
})
fs_table$`Dropped Features` <- sapply(lr_results$features, function(f) {
  selected <- strsplit(f, ";")[[1]]
  dropped <- setdiff(all_features, selected)
  if (length(dropped) == 0) return("(none)")
  paste(sort(dropped), collapse = ", ")
})

kable(fs_table, caption = "Features selected and dropped by each feature selection method.",
      booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position", "repeat_header"),
                font_size = 8) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "1.8cm") %>%
  column_spec(2, width = "1.2cm") %>%
  column_spec(3, width = "6cm") %>%
  column_spec(4, width = "5cm")
```

Table \@ref(tab:feature-selection-table) shows the features selected and dropped by each feature selection method. Elastic net and Boruta did not drop any variables, while forward stepwise selection and lasso were the most aggressive, retaining `r lr_results$n_features[1]` and `r lr_results$n_features[2]` features respectively. Both stepwise and lasso dropped gender, suggesting it has limited predictive value for ICU discharge once clinical variables are accounted for. Across all methods, SOFA scores and GCS subscores were universally retained, consistent with existing literature indicating that organ dysfunction and neurological status are strong predictors of ICU discharge. Regarding vital signs, maximal values were preferentially dropped as redundant relative to other summary statistics: both stepwise and lasso dropped the maximum values of diastolic blood pressure, glucose, and oxygen saturation, while lasso additionally dropped the maximum values of mean arterial pressure, systolic blood pressure, and temperature.

# Predictive modeling

## Methodology 

The following models were fit to the data:

- *Logistic regression:* A linear model that models the log-odds of ICU discharge as a linear combination of features, using maximum likelihood estimation to find coefficients.
- *Decision tree:* Partitions the high-dimensional feature space by selecting splits that maximize class purity at each node.
- *SVM:* Finds the optimal hyperplane that maximally separates discharged and deceased patients in feature space, using a kernel to map data.
- *XGBoost:* Sequentially fits shallow decision trees, incorporating subsampling and regularization to build a robust, generalizable learner.
- *AdaBoost:* Sequentially fits weak decision stumps (one-level trees) by iteratively increasing the weight accorded to misclassified observations, aggregating them into a final strong learner via a weighted majority vote.
- **Random forest:** Builds an ensemble of independent, fully-grown decision trees on bootstrapped samples with random feature subsets at each split, reducing variance through majority-vote aggregation of predictions. Random forest is based on bagging, with trees built in parallel and independently of one another, and primarily reduces variance. This is the key distinction from AdaBoost and XGBoost, which build trees sequentially in a dependent fashion and primarily aim to reduce bias.

## Model results

Logistic regression models trained on training data using the four feature sets as well as the full list of candidate features had similar performance. All models had AUC 0.87, sensitivity 0.80, specificity 0.77, positive predictive value 0.97, and negative predictive value of 0.29. Table \@ref(tab:logistic-results) shows the test performance of logistic regression models trained with different feature selection strategies. Feature selection with stepwise or lasso regression managed to reduce the number of features to 26 (lasso) and 32 (stepwise) while maintaining similar performance.

```{r logistic-results, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
lr <- load_results("results/logistic_regression_results.csv")
lr_display <- lr[, c("feature_set", "n_features", "AUC",
                      "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")]
names(lr_display) <- c("Feature Set", "# Features", "AUC",
                        "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")
kable(lr_display,
      caption = "Test performance of logistic regression models by feature selection strategy.",
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

Table \@ref(tab:dt-results) shows the test performance of decision tree models. Similar to the logistic regression models, reduction in the number of features from the full set (38 features) to a reduced set (26 features in lasso, 32 in stepwise) did not compromise the performance of the decision trees. Although decision trees had a poorer AUC compared to logistic regression, they had consistently superior sensitivity and negative predictive value. This means that compared to logistic regression, a decision tree is more likely to detect those patients who have a positive outcome of being discharged. Also, if a decision tree were to predict a negative patient outcome (i.e. the prediction is that the patient dies), the decision tree has a higher chance of being correct as compared to logistic regression. However, this gain in sensitivity comes at the cost of lower specificity, meaning the decision tree is more likely to incorrectly predict discharge for patients who actually die in the ICU.

```{r dt-results, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
dt <- load_results("results/decision_tree_results.csv")
dt_display <- dt[, c("feature_set", "n_features",
                     "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")]
names(dt_display) <- c("Feature Set", "# Features",
                        "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")
kable(dt_display,
      caption = "Test performance of decision tree models by feature selection strategy.",
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

Tables \@ref(tab:svm-results), \@ref(tab:adaboost-results), and \@ref(tab:xgboost-results) show the test performance of SVM, AdaBoost, and XGBoost models, respectively. As far as these models are concerned, we see similar patterns in the effects of applying feature selection strategies as observed in the logistic regression and decision tree models. Feature selection reduced model complexity (number of features), which improves model utility (enabling faster inference and greater clinical usability, as less input data from patients is required) while maintaining similar performance across all metrics.

```{r svm-results, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
svm_res <- load_results("results/svm_results.csv")
svm_display <- svm_res[, c("feature_set", "n_features",
                            "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")]
names(svm_display) <- c("Feature Set", "# Features",
                         "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")
kable(svm_display,
      caption = "Test performance of SVM models by feature selection strategy.",
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

```{r adaboost-results, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
ada <- load_results("results/adaboost_results.csv")
ada_display <- ada[, c("feature_set", "n_features",
                        "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")]
names(ada_display) <- c("Feature Set", "# Features",
                         "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")
kable(ada_display,
      caption = "Test performance of AdaBoost models by feature selection strategy.",
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

```{r xgboost-results, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
xgb <- load_results("results/xgboost_results.csv")
xgb_display <- xgb[, c("feature_set", "n_features",
                         "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")]
names(xgb_display) <- c("Feature Set", "# Features",
                          "AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")
kable(xgb_display,
      caption = "Test performance of XGBoost models by feature selection strategy.",
      booktabs = TRUE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

## Model comparison

Table \@ref(tab:model-comparison) summarizes average test performance across feature sets for each model type. Figure \@ref(fig:roc-comparison) shows the ROC curves for each model type using the most parsimonious feature set (lasso, 26 features), showing generally superior performance of the tree-based models.

While decision tree has a lower AUC than logistic regression, it has superior sensitivity and negative predictive value. The choice of model may depend on clinical considerations. For example, a clinician who prioritizes sensitivity for predicting who will be discharged from the ICU (maybe because they wish to make post-discharge plans) may prefer the decision tree.

Notably, Random Forest achieved the best AUC, sensitivity, specificity, and NPV across all models, suggesting that the variance-reducing benefits of bagging are highly beneficial for predictive performance in this setting. As such, further hyperparameter tuning of a random forest model would be a promising direction for future work.

Unsurprisingly, AdaBoost and XGBoost also achieved strong AUC and sensitivity, outperforming logistic regression and decision tree models. However, this superior sensitivity for predicting ICU discharge comes at the cost of lower specificity. Most notably, the negative predictive values of AdaBoost (0.53) and XGBoost (0.64) were substantially higher than those of logistic regression (0.28) and the decision tree (0.35), suggesting that boosted models are considerably better at correctly predicting ICU death. The comparatively poor performance of logistic regression may arise from its assumption of a linear relationship between features and the log-odds, as well as its assumption of feature independence, both of which are likely violated given the clinical complexity of ICU data, where features may have complex relationships with the outcome of ICU discharge and may interact with one another. XGBoost and AdaBoost outperform the single decision tree by better balancing the bias-variance tradeoff through ensemble learning. Overall, boosted models deliver meaningfully higher performance, with modest gains in AUC and sensitivity and a substantial improvement in NPV.

```{r roc-comparison, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="ROC curves for the lasso-selected feature set (most parsimonious), evaluated on test data.", out.width="60%", fig.align="center"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/roc_comparison_lasso.png")
```

```{r model-comparison, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
lr <- load_results("results/logistic_regression_results.csv")
dt <- load_results("results/decision_tree_results.csv")
svm_comp <- load_results("results/svm_results.csv")
ada_comp <- load_results("results/adaboost_results.csv")
xgb_comp <- load_results("results/xgboost_results.csv")
rf_comp  <- load_results("results/random_forest_results.csv")

metrics <- c("AUC", "Sensitivity", "Specificity", "PPV", "NPV")

lr_avg  <- colMeans(lr[, metrics])
dt_avg  <- colMeans(dt[, metrics])
svm_avg <- colMeans(svm_comp[, metrics])
ada_avg <- colMeans(ada_comp[, metrics])
xgb_avg <- colMeans(xgb_comp[, metrics])
rf_avg  <- colMeans(rf_comp[, metrics])

comparison <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Logistic Regression", "Decision Tree", "SVM", "AdaBoost", "XGBoost", "Random Forest"),
  rbind(lr_avg, dt_avg, svm_avg, ada_avg, xgb_avg, rf_avg),
  row.names = NULL
)

kable(comparison,
      caption = "Average test performance by model type (across feature sets).",
      booktabs = TRUE, digits = 4) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "hold_position")
```

# Declaration

AI tools, specifically Anthropic Claude Chat and Anthropic Claude Code, were used for understanding technical documentation, assistance with coding especially repetitive tasks such as generation of code for unfamiliar model types according to the author's analytical plans, formatting and stylistics, and language editing.
